behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is handler-module interaction: signature verifier + HTTP response + queue forwarding gate. A unit test of only HMAC logic misses bugs where invalid requests still get enqueued. Real OS/network/subprocess is not required (in-process HTTP harness and fake queue are sufficient), and no AI/ML output is involved.
behaviors.test_description: Run the webhook handler in-process, send a request with mismatched `X-Signature-256`, assert HTTP 401 and assert queue `enqueue` was not called / queue store unchanged.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected System: behavior text does not require real network/OS effects; rejected Agentic/Workflow: no model decisions.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is explicit cross-module ordering/state handoff (HTTP handler ↔ persistent queue). Unit is too low because the failure appears at the boundary. It does not require real external network or subprocess; a local persistence-backed queue in the same process can expose ordering regressions.
behaviors.test_description: Instrument handler and persistent queue write completion; send a valid request and assert queue write commit timestamp/order occurs before HTTP 200 is emitted.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected System: no behavior text requiring real external infra beyond local module wiring/persistence; rejected Agentic/Workflow: no AI steps.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Failure is asynchronous orchestration between handler, queue, and downstream worker trigger. Unit tests of one function can miss await/Promise coupling bugs. Real network/OS is not necessary; in-process server plus deliberately slow downstream stub reproduces the failure.
behaviors.test_description: Use an in-process app with downstream processor delayed (e.g., unresolved promise for several seconds). Send a valid event and assert response returns immediately while downstream remains unfinished.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected System: no requirement for real HTTP over network or OS primitives; rejected Agentic/Workflow: no model output dependence.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: Behavior is wall-clock SLA under concurrency, sensitive to event-loop scheduling, socket handling, and real timing effects. In-process fakes/timers can hide starvation/queueing failures that appear only with real HTTP and concurrent load. No AI/ML dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real service listener, pre-load slow concurrent downstream work, then send webhook requests over real HTTP and measure end-to-end latency; assert each response is received within 3 seconds.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected Integration: behavior text explicitly requires wall-clock and concurrent conditions where mocked timing can mask real failures; rejected Agentic/Workflow: no model decisions.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Dedup correctness is at state handoff boundary (ID tracking/persistence + enqueue path). A pure unit test can miss duplicate writes caused by repository/queue interaction. Real external infra is unnecessary if using local persistence/in-process modules.
behaviors.test_description: Submit the same event ID twice through the handler and assert only one persistent queue record exists and second attempt is treated as duplicate (no second write).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected System: no explicit need for external network/OS behavior; rejected Agentic/Workflow: no AI dependence.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is handler-to-queue error propagation and HTTP mapping. Unit-only response mapping is insufficient if queue-full signals are misinterpreted across modules. Real OS/network/subprocess not required; queue-full can be produced by an in-process queue module/fake implementing real interface.
behaviors.test_description: Configure queue component to return `FULL` on enqueue; send valid webhook and assert HTTP 503 plus `Retry-After` header value/presence.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected System: behavior is protocol mapping, not real infra behavior; rejected Agentic/Workflow: no model steps.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This validates real queue implementation plus persistence/config interaction, beyond single-function logic. It needs module wiring with local storage semantics; no external network or subprocess is required. Mocking queue internals would hide real capacity-enforcement bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue with small capacity, enqueue up to limit, then enqueue one more and assert a concrete rejection/error and no extra persisted record.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected System: can be reproduced with local in-process persistence without real external systems; rejected Agentic/Workflow: no AI/ML output dependence.
